title: Data Types & Operations
description: Python's type system has subtleties that trip up even experienced engineers in FAANG interviews. Mutability, identity vs equality, interning, and the hash contract are all fair game.

sections.title: Mutability — The Foundation
sections.description:
Mutability determines whether an object's value can change after creation. Mutable: list, dict, set, bytearray. Immutable: int, float, str, tuple, frozenset, bytes.

Why it matters: Immutable objects are hashable (can be dict keys / set elements). Mutable defaults in function signatures are a notorious Python gotcha.
sections.codeBlock:
# The mutable default argument trap — a classic FAANG gotcha
def bad_append(item, lst=[]):   # lst is created ONCE at definition
    lst.append(item)
    return lst

bad_append(1)  # [1]
bad_append(2)  # [1, 2] — unexpected! same list reused

# Fix: use None as sentinel
def good_append(item, lst=None):
    if lst is None: lst = []    # fresh list every call
    lst.append(item)
    return lst

# Tuple is immutable — but can contain mutable objects
t = ([1, 2], [3, 4])
t[0].append(99)   # OK — we're mutating the list, not the tuple
print(t)          # ([1, 2, 99], [3, 4])
# t[0] = [5]     # TypeError — can't replace the reference

# frozenset — immutable set, hashable
fs = frozenset({1, 2, 3})
d = {fs: 'a set as a key'}   # works — frozenset is hashable

sections.title: Identity vs Equality: is vs ==
sections.description:
== checks value equality (calls __eq__). is checks object identity (same memory address). These are different — and confusing them is a red flag in interviews.

Integer interning: CPython caches integers in range [-5, 256] — a is b will be True for small ints. This is an implementation detail, not a language guarantee.
sections.codeBlock:
# is vs == — never use 'is' for value comparison
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
print(a == b)    # True — same values
print(a is b)    # False — different objects

c = a
print(a is c)    # True — same reference

# Integer interning (CPython implementation detail)
x = 256
y = 256
print(x is y)    # True — cached in [-5, 256]

x = 257
y = 257
print(x is y)    # False (usually) — not cached

# String interning — also implementation-specific
s1 = 'hello'
s2 = 'hello'
print(s1 is s2)  # True — interned (short strings, valid identifiers)

s1 = 'hello world'  # contains space
s2 = 'hello world'
print(s1 is s2)  # False — may not be interned

# Always use 'is' for: None, True, False
if x is None: ...    # correct
if x == None: ...    # works but bad style — triggers __eq__

sections.title: The Hash Contract
sections.description:
For an object to be used as a dict key or set element, it must be hashable: it must implement __hash__ and __eq__, and the invariant a == b → hash(a) == hash(b) must hold. The reverse is not required (hash collisions are allowed).

Python automatically sets __hash__ = None if you define __eq__ without __hash__, making the object unhashable.

sections.title: Type Conversion & Numeric Types
sections.description: Python has arbitrary-precision integers (no overflow!), IEEE 754 double-precision floats (15–16 sig digits), and the decimal and fractions modules for exact arithmetic. In FAANG interviews, knowing Python integers don't overflow is a competitive advantage.
sections.codeBlock:
import sys

# Python ints have unlimited precision — no overflow
big = 2 ** 1000    # works perfectly
print(len(str(big)))  # 302 digits

# Float gotcha — floating point is not exact
print(0.1 + 0.2)          # 0.30000000000000004
print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)   # False!
print(abs(0.1 + 0.2 - 0.3) < 1e-9)  # True — use epsilon comparison

# Integer division
print(7 // 2)   # 3 — floor division (always rounds toward -inf)
print(-7 // 2)  # -4 (not -3!) — floor toward negative infinity
print(-7 % 2)   # 1 (not -1!) — same sign as divisor in Python

# Type checking
print(isinstance(True, int))  # True — bool is a subclass of int!
print(True + True)            # 2
print(True * 5)               # 5

# Bit operations (common in interview tricks)
print(bin(13))        # '0b1101'
print(13 & 7)         # 5  (AND)
print(13 | 7)         # 15 (OR)
print(13 ^ 7)         # 10 (XOR)
print(13 >> 1)        # 6  (right shift = floor div by 2)
print(13 << 1)        # 26 (left shift = multiply by 2)

sections.title: String Internals & Operations
sections.description: Python strings are immutable sequences of Unicode code points. String concatenation in a loop is O(N²) — each + creates a new string. Use str.join() for O(N) concatenation.
sections.codeBlock:
# O(N²) — avoid in loops
result = ''
for ch in 'hello world':
    result += ch   # creates new string each iteration

# O(N) — correct way
result = ''.join(['h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o'])

# String methods — all return new strings (immutable)
s = '  Hello, World!  '
print(s.strip())           # 'Hello, World!'
print(s.lower())           # '  hello, world!  '
print(s.split(','))        # ['  Hello', ' World!  ']
print('abc' * 3)           # 'abcabcabc'
print('hello'[::-1])       # 'olleh' — slice reversal

# f-strings (Python 3.6+) — most efficient for formatting
name, score = 'Alice', 98.5
print(f'{name} scored {score:.1f}%')  # 'Alice scored 98.5%'
print(f'{score:010.2f}')   # '0000098.50' — zero-padded
print(f'{42:b}')           # '101010' — binary format

# ord/chr for char-to-int conversion (common in interview solutions)
print(ord('a'))    # 97
print(chr(97))     # 'a'
print(ord('A'))    # 65
# Lowercase index trick:
idx = ord(ch) - ord('a')  # maps a→0, b→1, ..., z→25

sections.title: 6. Staff+ Patterns — Memory Layout, Interning Internals, and Bit Manipulation
sections.description:
Memory layout: Python objects have overhead. An int is ~28 bytes (ob_base, ob_digit array). A float is ~24 bytes. A list is ~56 bytes + 8 bytes per element pointer + the objects themselves. Knowing this helps debug memory bloat in production services.

Interning internals: CPython interns (1) all ints in [-5, 256], (2) short strings that look like identifiers (re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$', s)), and (3) empty tuple. sys.intern() forces interning — use for high-volume string comparisons (deduplication saves memory and speeds up dict lookups).

Bit manipulation patterns: FAANG Staff+ expects fluency: x & (x-1) == 0 (power of 2), n & -n (lowest set bit), x ^ y (find differences), ~x & mask (bit flip in range). Python's arbitrary-precision ints make bit tricks unbounded — int.bit_length(), int.bit_count() (Python 3.8+).

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your service caches 10M user IDs (strings). Memory is too high. What do you do?' — Intern the strings (sys.intern()) for deduplication, or store as ints if the IDs are numeric strings. For guaranteed dedup across process restarts, use a WeakSet of interned strings.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Memory overhead ===
import sys

print('int:', sys.getsizeof(1))          # 28
print('float:', sys.getsizeof(1.0))      # 24
print('bool:', sys.getsizeof(True))      # 28 (bool is int subclass)
print('None:', sys.getsizeof(None))      # 16
print('tuple:', sys.getsizeof((1,2,3)))  # 64
print('list:', sys.getsizeof([1,2,3]))   # 88 + 28*3 for ints

# === Interning for memory/performance ===
import sys

s1 = sys.intern('my_very_long_identifier_string_123')
s2 = sys.intern('my_very_long_identifier_string_123')
print(s1 is s2)  # True — single object in memory

# 100M interned strings share memory; 100M non-interned are 100M separate objects

# === Bit manipulation ===
def is_power_of_two(n: int) -> bool:
    return n > 0 and (n & (n - 1)) == 0

def lowest_set_bit(n: int) -> int:
    return n & -n

def popcount(n: int) -> int:
    return n.bit_count()  # Python 3.8+, CPU instruction on modern hardware

def count_bits_to_flip(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return (a ^ b).bit_count()

# Bit operations for interview problems
# Subset enumeration via bitmask:
nums = ['a', 'b', 'c']
n = len(nums)
for mask in range(1 << n):
    subset = [nums[i] for i in range(n) if mask & (1 << i)]
    print(subset)  # [], ['a'], ['b'], ['a','b'], ...

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Python Data Model — Official docs on Python's type system and object model
  • Built-in Types — Python standard library type documentation
  • Mutable vs Immutable Objects — Real Python deep dive with interview-relevant examples
  • PEP 8 — Python style guide: type conventions and naming
  • Fluent Python (2nd ed.) — Chapters 2-6: data structures, mutability, protocol of sequences and hashing

details:
Interning and the is trap: Never rely on object identity for equality in production code. CPython interns small integers and some strings as an optimization, but this is an implementation detail that may differ across Python versions, PyPy, or Jython. The only safe uses of 'is' are comparisons to None, True, and False.

Python's None, True, False: These are singleton objects — there is exactly one None, one True, one False in any Python process. Always use 'is None' not '== None', and 'if x' not 'if x == True'.

Type hints (Python 3.9+): Use built-in types directly: list[int], dict[str, int], tuple[int, ...], set[str]. No need to import from typing for basic generics. Union types use X | Y syntax. Optional[X] = X | None. These are important for senior-level Python positions at FAANG.
